data.title: USA Rare Earth CEO says U.S. in 'early innings' of rare earths independence from China
data.description:
The U.S. is in "the very early innings" of independence from China for rare earth metals, USA Rare Earth (USAR) CEO Barbara Humpton said Wednesday in a Bloomberg interview.

"China set this as an objective decades ago," the CEO said. "They declared the Middle East has oil, China has rare earths, and now we've seen how that monopoly has been used for statecraft."

USA Rare Earth (USAR) has no operating mine but has swiftly vaulted into multibillion-dollar dealmaking, and its shares have surged ~35% in the three days since the company announced its $2.8B acquisition of Brazil's Serra Verde Group, which owns the only producing rare earths mine in Brazil, operating a large ionic clay deposit capable of producing key magnet rare earths, including scarcer heavy elements.

Humpton said USA Rare Earth (USAR) plans to increase domestic production through mining operations in Texas, a separation facility in Colorado, and a magnet-making plant in Stillwater, Oklahoma, adding that the company is focused on growing organically to grow the value chain, benefiting from demand from magnet makers searching for a reliable supply.

Analysts at Canaccord reiterated their Buy rating for USA Rare Earth (USAR) and raised their stock price target to $32 from $29, noting the Serra Verde deal secures supply so the company can expand production at the new Stillwater facility, which is expected to begin commercial production at the heavy rare earth element-rich Round Top deposit in Texas in 2028.

data.title: Fed chair nominee Warsh's inflation strategy could backfire - BofA
data.description:
Kevin Warsh, U.S. President Donald Trump’s nominee for Federal Reserve chair, told lawmakers this week he wants to change how the central bank measures inflation, though economists warn the shift could complicate his approach to monetary policy.

During his Senate confirmation hearing Tuesday, Warsh said he prefers “trimmed average” inflation measures over the Fed’s traditional core price index for personal consumption expenditures (PCE). The trimmed approach removes extreme price movements to reveal underlying inflation trends.

“What I’m most interested in is: What’s the underlying inflation rate?” Warsh said. “We take out all of the tail-risks, all of the one-off items, and we ask ourselves whether the generalized change in prices is having second-order effects on the economy.”

Bank of America economist Aditya Bhave reportedly noted that while trimmed measures currently show softer inflation—a 2.3% mean compared with core PCE at 3%—the strategy carries risks.

Bhave warned that food and energy prices, currently excluded from core PCE, could still influence trimmed readings during volatile periods. Historical data shows trimmed-median inflation exceeded core PCE in 2019 and 2020, which would have encouraged a more hawkish Fed stance.

“To preserve Fed credibility and avoid optics of cherry picking, Warsh will need to stick with his preferred metrics even when they are outpacing the core,” Bhave said, CNBC reported.

data.title: Amneal anticipates deleveraging below 3x by 2028 as it outlines $4.3B-$4.5B 2030 revenue target following Kashiv deal
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Amneal Pharmaceuticals (AMRX) Q1 2026

Management View

  • "Today is a defining moment for Amneal." (Co-Founder, Co-CEO, President & Director Chirag Patel) "This morning, we announced that Amneal agrees to acquire Kashiv BioSciences, creating a fully integrated global biosimilars leader and positioning Amneal to become the #1 affordable medicines company in the United States." (Co-Founder, Co-CEO, President & Director Patel)

  • "Q1 marked another consecutive quarter of strong top and bottom line growth with revenue up 4%, adjusted EBITDA up 19% and EPS up 29%." (Co-Founder, Co-CEO, President & Director Patel) "Our strong start of the year, combined with growth of existing and new products, gives us confidence to raise our stand-alone guidance for 2026." (Co-Founder, Co-CEO, President & Director Patel)

  • "The upfront value of $750 million is a 50-50 mix of cash and equity." (Executive VP & CFO Anastasios Konidaris) "The equity portion translates to approximately $29 million of Amneal shares, representing 8% equity dilution." (Executive VP & CFO Konidaris)

  • "We expect $400 million to $500 million in cumulative financial synergies over time." (Executive VP & CFO Konidaris) "First, we're now capturing full economics from partnered assets by eliminating milestones and profit-sharing obligations that existed as part of prior licensing deals." (Executive VP & CFO Konidaris)

  • "Kashiv is a biologics platform built over 12 years with more than $900 million invested, 600-plus employees and 4 R&D and manufacturing sites." (Co-Founder, Co-CEO & Director Chintu Patel) "Drug substance capacity is expected to scale from 26,000 liter in 2026 to 75,000 liter by 2028." (Co-Founder, Co-CEO & Director Patel)

Outlook

  • "On a combined basis, including Kashiv, our 2026 view remains largely unchanged aside from a small impact to cash flow related to near-term transaction and integration costs." (Executive VP & CFO Konidaris) "Importantly, we're maintaining the higher adjusted EBITDA and EPS outlook." (Executive VP & CFO Konidaris)

  • "We expect the combined company's net debt leverage ratio at the end of 2026 to be 3.7x adjusted EBITDA." (Executive VP & CFO Konidaris) "We expect to resume our deleveraging in 2027 and expect our net leverage ratio to be 3x below adjusted EBITDA -- net debt adjusted EBITDA by 2028." (Executive VP & CFO Konidaris)

  • "By 2030, we expect revenues to have grown by approximately $1.2 billion or 40% over 2026 and EPS up by approximately $0.70 or 70% over 2026." (Executive VP & CFO Konidaris)

  • "If you think about 2030, for example, the guidance we're providing is between $4.3 billion and $4.5 billion." (Executive VP & CFO Konidaris) "Probably about $1 billion -- a little over $1 billion, $1 billion to $1.3 billion, that's going to be biosimilars." (Executive VP & CFO Konidaris)

Financial Results

  • "Total net revenues in the first quarter of $723 million grew 4%." (Executive VP & CFO Konidaris) "Q1 2026 adjusted EBITDA of $202 million, up 19% and Q1 adjusted EPS of $0.27, up 29%." (Executive VP & CFO Konidaris)

  • "Q1 Affordable Medicines revenue of $423 million grew 2%." (Executive VP & CFO Konidaris) "These high-margin products drove Q1 segment gross margin to 47.3%, up 320 basis points versus Q1 of 2025." (Executive VP & CFO Konidaris)

  • "Q1 Specialty revenue of $133 million grew 23%." (Executive VP & CFO Konidaris) "First quarter CREXONT revenue of $21 million reflects continued strong market uptake." (Executive VP & CFO Konidaris)

  • "Q1 revenues of $166 million declined by $6 million or 4% as strong growth in our government channel was offset by expected decline in the low-margin distribution channel." (Executive VP & CFO Konidaris) "We're happy to report that AvKARE's gross margin in the quarter grew by 690 basis points versus first quarter last year." (Executive VP & CFO Konidaris)

Q&A

  • Matthew Dellatorre, Goldman Sachs Group, Inc., Research Division: asked about portfolio construction and international biosimilars; Co-Founder, Co-CEO, President & Director Patel replied, "Our portfolio would be probably 70% would be niche, about 30% would be the large molecule that we must have to offer a complete package to the customers," and added, "We don't intend to have boots on grounds in Europe or South America or Canada, that's not where we are focused on." (Co-Founder, Co-CEO, President & Director Patel)

  • Leszek Sulewski, Truist Securities, Inc., Research Division: asked about capacity expansion spend and gross margin trajectory; Co-Founder, Co-CEO & Director Patel said, "It's about $30 million, $50 million a year, we'll be spending for next 2, 3 years on the CapEx to get to the 75,000 liter," and Executive VP & CFO Konidaris said, "We will finish 2026 at about 45%." (Co-Founder, Co-CEO & Director Patel; Executive VP & CFO Konidaris)

  • David Amsellem, Piper Sandler & Co., Research Division: questioned Kashiv insider ownership and why give 2030 targets; Executive VP & CFO Konidaris said ownership "has been owned by the Amneal Group," and added, "For us to provide long-term guidance, we had to feel pretty confident on our ability to deliver on those commitments." (Executive VP & CFO Konidaris)

  • Christopher Schott, JPMorgan Chase & Co, Research Division: asked about biosimilar competition and lanreotide; Co-Founder, Co-CEO, President & Director Patel said, "We see a few companies will come from India, a few maybe from China," and on lanreotide added, "We have requested FDA to expedite the approval and they're working on it." (Co-Founder, Co-CEO, President & Director Patel)

  • Glen Santangelo, Barclays Bank PLC, Research Division: pressed on vertical integration complexity and 2027 EBITDA; Co-Founder, Co-CEO, President & Director Patel said, "Yes, it is complex," while Executive VP & CFO Konidaris responded, "We feel great about growing EBITDA 9%, 10%, even absorbing a strategic deal, which is going to have some dilution next year until it becomes accretive in 2028." (Co-Founder, Co-CEO, President & Director Patel; Executive VP & CFO Konidaris)

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analyst tone was slightly negative at points where assumptions were challenged, including Barclays framing that 2027 EBITDA guidance "is a decent amount below what the Street was already forecasting" and asking whether vertical integration increases operational risk. (Glen Santangelo, Barclays Bank PLC, Research Division)

  • Management tone was confident in prepared remarks and generally firm in Q&A, including "Today is a defining moment for Amneal" and "we're maintaining the higher adjusted EBITDA and EPS outlook," while acknowledging near-term integration effects as "a small impact to cash flow." (Co-Founder, Co-CEO, President & Director Chirag Patel; Executive VP & CFO Anastasios Konidaris)

  • Versus the prior quarter’s emphasis on 2026 execution, the current call added higher-conviction long-range framing, including "By 2030, we expect revenues to have grown by approximately $1.2 billion or 40% over 2026" and providing an explicit 2030 revenue range. (Executive VP & CFO Konidaris)

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The current call centered on the Kashiv acquisition and biosimilars vertical integration, with management stating it "creates fully integrated global biosimilars leader" and provides a pipeline of "more than 20 biosimilars programs." (Co-Founder, Co-CEO, President & Director Chirag Patel)

  • The previous quarter positioned vertical integration as an objective rather than a signed transaction, stating, "Strategically, our goal remains to be vertically integrated in biosimilars across development, manufacturing and commercialization." (Co-Founder, Co-CEO, President & Director Chirag Patel)

  • Guidance language expanded from near-term 2026 outlook in Q4 to multi-year targets in Q1; previously management guided "total company revenue of $3.05 billion to $3.15 billion" for 2026, while the current call added 2030 targets and leverage milestones tied to the acquisition. (Executive VP & CFO Anastasios Konidaris)

Risks and Concerns

  • "The transaction will be funded by cash on hand as well as some additional debt." (Executive VP & CFO Konidaris) "Amneal will fund operations between signing and closing of the deal." (Executive VP & CFO Konidaris)

  • "It's just to kind of be able the sustainability of 510 basis points is kind of hard to keep repeating quarter after quarter." (Executive VP & CFO Konidaris) "We have a little bit more modest gross margin expansion for the rest of the year." (Executive VP & CFO Konidaris)

  • "The gating item is only the FDA approval." (Co-Founder, Co-CEO, President & Director Chirag Patel)

Final Takeaway

Management presented the Kashiv acquisition as a shift to a vertically integrated biosimilars model, describing the deal as "structured to create value from day 1" with "$400 million to $500 million" of expected cumulative synergies, while keeping a stated deleveraging path to "below 3x" by 2028. Alongside the transaction narrative, management highlighted preliminary Q1 results of $723 million in revenue and $0.27 adjusted EPS, and laid out longer-range targets including 2030 revenue of $4.3 billion to $4.5 billion with biosimilars of about $1.0 billion to $1.3 billion.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Cadence partners with TSMC to speed up chip development with agentic design
data.description:
Cadence Design Systems (CDNS) has expanded its partnership with Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing (TSM) to enhance artificial intelligence chip development.

Shares of Cadence were up 1.6% during afternoon trading on Wednesday.

The expanded collaboration will deliver IP, signoff-ready, end-to-end design infrastructure, and advanced, certified flows for AI silicon on Taiwan Semiconductor's N3, N2, A16, and A14 process technologies. It is expected to lead to reduced iterations and accelerate the chipmaking process.

Cadence has added agentic AI to the chip design process. Chipmakers such as Nvidia (NVDA) and Arm (ARM) are utilizing these developments.

"The increasing scale and complexity of next-generation AI silicon require a reinvented approach to design that integrates accelerated computing and agentic AI at every stage of the chip design cycle," said Tim Costa, vice president and general manager of computational engineering at Nvidia. "By collaborating with Cadence, Nvidia is helping advance the EDA tools necessary for its design teams and the global semiconductor ecosystem to optimize performance and accelerate the delivery of the world's most sophisticated AI architectures."

"Collaboration within the ecosystem—including between leading design and manufacturing partners such as Cadence and TSMC—plays an important role in enabling the next generation of Arm-based infrastructure for AI and HPC deployments," said Eddie Ramirez, vice president of go-to-market for the Cloud AI business unit at Arm.

data.title: M/I Homes targets about 5% average community count growth in 2026 amid affordability pressure
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: M/I Homes (MHO) Q1 2026

Management View

  • “We had a very solid first quarter, highlighted by revenues of $921 million, pretax income of $89 million and a strong pretax income return of 10%.” (Chairman, President & CEO Robert Schottenstein)
  • “Market conditions slightly shifted at the end of February and into March as events in the Middle East pushed mortgage rates up higher, impacted gas prices and contributed to further market uncertainty.” (Chairman, President & CEO Schottenstein)
  • “Mortgage rate buydowns continue to be an important part of our sales strategy.” (Chairman, President & CEO Schottenstein)
  • “We sold 2,350 homes during the quarter.” (Chairman, President & CEO Schottenstein)
  • “We ended the first quarter with 230 communities and are on track to grow our community count in 2026 by an average of about 5% from 2025.” (Chairman, President & CEO Schottenstein)
  • “Our balance sheet continues to be very strong… 0 borrowings under our $900 million unsecured revolving credit facility and over $750 million in cash.” (Chairman, President & CEO Schottenstein)
  • “Our first quarter gross margin was 22%, down 390 basis points year-over-year due to higher home buyer incentives and higher lot costs versus the same period a year ago.” (Executive VP, CFO & Director Phillip Creek)
  • “We spent $50 million in the first quarter repurchasing our stock and have $170 million remaining under our Board authorization.” (Executive VP, CFO & Director Creek)
  • “Our mortgage operation captured 96% of our business in the first quarter, up from 92% last year.” (President & CEO of M/I Financial Derek Klutch)

Outlook

  • “We ended the first quarter with 230 communities and are on track to grow our community count in 2026 by an average of about 5% from 2025.” (Chairman, President & CEO Schottenstein)
  • “Conditions right now are marked with uncertainty.” (Chairman, President & CEO Schottenstein)
  • “I've been pleased with our traffic through the first quarter and through April so far.” (Chairman, President & CEO Schottenstein)
  • “We don't give margin guidance.” (Chairman, President & CEO Schottenstein)

Financial Results

  • “Our first quarter total revenue decreased 6% to $921 million, and pretax income decreased 39% to $89.2 million.” (Chairman, President & CEO Schottenstein)
  • “Our earnings per diluted share for the quarter was $2.55 per share compared to $3.98 last year.” (Executive VP, CFO & Director Creek)
  • “Our average closing price for the first quarter was $459,000.” (Executive VP, CFO & Director Creek)
  • “Our first quarter SG&A expenses were 12.7% of revenue versus 11.5% a year ago.” (Executive VP, CFO & Director Creek)
  • “During the quarter, we generated $99 million of EBITDA compared to $154 million a year ago.” (Executive VP, CFO & Director Creek)
  • “We ended the first quarter with no borrowings under our $900 million credit facility and had a cash balance of $767 million.” (Executive VP, CFO & Director Creek)
  • “During 2026 first quarter, we spent $79 million on land purchases and $104 million on land development for a total of $183 million.” (Executive VP, CFO & Director Creek)
  • “Our mortgage and title operations achieved pretax income of $14.1 million.” (President & CEO of M/I Financial Klutch)

Q&A

  • “Have you received any form of communication regarding any cost increases from your vendors because of fuel prices…?” (Natalie Kulasekere, Zelman & Associates LLC) “The short answer is yes… So far, there hasn't been much impact.” (Chairman, President & CEO Schottenstein)
  • “So is there anything specific that drove this lower this quarter?” (Kulasekere, Zelman & Associates LLC) “I actually think it's more mix than anything else… I'd expect our average sales price to be at this level, maybe slightly higher.” (Chairman, President & CEO Schottenstein)
  • “Are most of your intra-quarter order closings coming from the Smart Series…?” (Kenneth Zener, Seaport Research Partners) “Not necessarily… I don't think there's really any discernible difference.” (Chairman, President & CEO Schottenstein)
  • “Can you talk to how those different regions are impacting the margins.” (Zener, Seaport Research Partners) “Our margins have held up better in our Midwest markets than in our Florida markets… the West Coast of Florida… appears to be the most challenging right now.” (Chairman, President & CEO Schottenstein)
  • “Could you talk about the increase year-on-year in the lots from the North…?” (Jay McCanless, Citizens JMP Securities) “We have a lot of opportunity to grow… 5% to 10% a year for the foreseeable future.” (Chairman, President & CEO Schottenstein)
  • “What the gross margin looks like in backlog…?” (McCanless, Citizens JMP Securities) “It really hasn't changed much… the backlog is not that big.” (Executive VP, CFO & Director Creek)
  • “Did you increase or lean into certain incentives more in March…?” (Buck Horne, Raymond James) “What we have led with… is a 4 7/8 rate… and… a long-term rate lock a rate in the very, very low 5s.” (Chairman, President & CEO Schottenstein)
  • “Is there a possibility that you'd kind of increase… the buybacks…?” (Horne, Raymond James) “We talk about it with our Board… I don't really see any change, but it's possible.” (Chairman, President & CEO Schottenstein)

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts: slightly negative to neutral, with questions pressing on cost creep, margin mix, incentives, and visibility (“do you think it's something that you could negotiate,” “how those different regions are impacting the margins,” “potential carryforward to second quarter margins”). (Kulasekere, Zener, Horne)
  • Management: slightly positive but cautious, repeatedly emphasizing uncertainty while defending execution (“Conditions right now are marked with uncertainty,” “I think housing is holding up pretty damn well,” and “we don't give margin guidance”). (Chairman, President & CEO Schottenstein)
  • Versus last quarter: management shifted from “we remain very confident” and seasonal “traffic start to pick up” toward more explicit macro uncertainty tied to geopolitical events (“events in the Middle East pushed mortgage rates up higher”). (Chairman, President & CEO Schottenstein)

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • Q1 emphasized geopolitical-driven rate volatility and fuel-related vendor conversations (“The issue of increased fuel has come up,” “events in the Middle East pushed mortgage rates up higher”), while Q4 focused on inventory/warranty charges and year-end clean-up to “start 2026 with all cylinders as strong as they can possibly be.” (Chairman, President & CEO Schottenstein)
  • Incentives remained centered on sub-5% fixed-rate buydowns in both quarters, with Q1 adding more detail on a consistent “4 7/8 rate” for near-term deliveries and “very, very low 5s” for longer locks. (Chairman, President & CEO Schottenstein)
  • Analysts stayed focused on margin drivers and spec/to-be-built mix in both quarters, with Q1 sharpening the discussion around regional margin dispersion and whether March volatility could pressure forward margins. (Zener, Horne)

Risks and Concerns

  • “Affordability and even consumer confidence… general uncertainty and volatility in the broader economy” were cited as ongoing demand headwinds. (Chairman, President & CEO Schottenstein)
  • “Higher home buyer incentives and higher lot costs” were cited as the key drivers of year-over-year gross margin pressure. (Executive VP, CFO & Director Creek)
  • “The West Coast of Florida… appears to be the most challenging right now.” (Chairman, President & CEO Schottenstein)
  • “Yes” to vendor fuel-cost communications, with management noting “so far, there hasn't been much impact,” alongside an emphasis on longstanding trade relationships and prior “internal cost reduction effort.” (Chairman, President & CEO Schottenstein)

Final Takeaway

Management framed Q1 as “very solid” despite affordability and macro volatility, highlighting contract growth, a gross margin anchored by incentives, and a balance sheet with no revolver borrowings and substantial cash. Executives described mortgage buydowns—especially a “4 7/8” fixed-rate offer on near-term inventory—as central to sustaining pace, while also pointing to a mixed regional margin backdrop where Midwest held up better than parts of Florida. The company reiterated plans to expand community count by about 5% in 2026 and maintained an active repurchase program, while declining to provide margin guidance amid “uncertainty.”

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Pegasystems outlines plan for Pega Cloud ACV to reach 75%+ of total ACV as Blueprint drives second-half conversions
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Pegasystems (PEGA) Q1 2026

Management View

  • “In mission-critical enterprises, AI increases the value of platforms that are architected for predictability, governance, interoperability and continuous change, and that's us.” (Founder, CEO & Chairman of the Board Alan Trefler)
  • “Since we last spoke, we introduced new vibe coding tooling into Pega Blueprint.” (CEO Trefler)
  • “Pega Cloud revenue in the first quarter of 2026 increased year-over-year from $151 million to $205 million.” (COO & CFO Kenneth Stillwell)
  • “Pega Cloud ACV grew 29% year-over-year as reported, and 27% in constant currency to just over $900 million, an over $200 million jump.” (CFO Stillwell)
  • “Free cash flow reached $207 million in Q1 of 2026, marking a strong start to the year.” (CFO Stillwell)
  • “In Q1, we returned more than 80% of our free cash flow to shareholders, repurchasing 3.5 million shares for $167 million under our repurchase program and paying $5 million in quarterly dividends.” (CFO Stillwell)

Outlook

  • “We provide full year guidance at the start of the year, and we typically do not issue quarterly guidance or update our outlook during the year.” (COO & CFO Kenneth Stillwell)
  • “Our renewal portfolio is back-end loaded this year, which means we expect to have higher level of business activity in the second half of the year.” (CFO Stillwell)
  • “We expect this new pipeline will begin converting into ACV in the second half of the year as deals progress through the sales cycle with a faster motion, thanks to Blueprint.” (CFO Stillwell)
  • “We expect term license revenue to be more heavily weighted towards the second half of 2026.” (CFO Stillwell)
  • “We expect it to continue as Pega Cloud ACV scales to 75% or more of our total ACV over time.” (CFO Stillwell)

Financial Results

  • “Pega Cloud ACV now represents about 56% of total ACV.” (COO & CFO Kenneth Stillwell)
  • “Our focus on growing Pega Cloud puts pressure on both term and maintenance ACV as well as revenue.” (CFO Stillwell)
  • “As of March 31, 2026, our shares outstanding decreased from the end of 2025 by 1.6 million shares.” (CFO Stillwell)

Q&A

  • Alexei Gogolev, JPMorgan Chase & Co: “would you mind providing a bit more color on acceleration of ACV growth through the year?”; COO & CFO Stillwell: “our renewal cycle is tipped towards the back end of 2026” and “the conversion of that pipeline will grow the opportunity tends to sit towards the back end of the year as well.”
  • Alexei Gogolev, JPMorgan Chase & Co: “update us on the trends from Q1 in terms of agentic adoption?”; Founder, CEO & Chairman Trefler: “there are some people… they think that they will be at their most successful when they use the LLMs for as much as possible… that's just crazy.”
  • Raimo Lenschow, Barclays: new logo focus and whether maintenance weakness is indicative; COO & CFO Stillwell: “you will see maintenance go down over time, and you will see term license be flat to down as well.”
  • Steven Enders, Citi: ACV/net new color and whether Q1 affected the year; COO & CFO Stillwell: “the constant currency growth in Q1 was somewhere around $20 million” and “the year is not that different than the way that I envisioned it playing out.”
  • Rishi Jaluria, RBC: geopolitical/government impacts; COO & CFO Stillwell: “there were a few deals and a renewal or 2 that actually did slip out of Q1” and “we don't believe that those are like lost deals.”
  • Devin Au, KeyBanc: regional revenue downtick; COO & CFO Stillwell: “That's solely just the timing of term license revenue.”
  • Patrick Walravens, Citizens JMP: “What is it going to be bad?”; Founder, CEO & Chairman Trefler: “there are what I would describe as point features that massively changed or gone away” and “a lot of the low-end workflow companies… have really… suffered in the market.”
  • Mark Schappel, Loop Capital: AI-driven pipeline mix; COO & CFO Stillwell: “almost all of our new pipeline growth is connected to a use of Blueprint in some way.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts were neutral to slightly negative, pressing on seasonality/back-half dependence, maintenance pressure, macro/geopolitics, and whether Q1 performance changed the full-year view; the tone is reflected in questions such as “did this… change how you think about what the ACV growth for the year will look like?” (Steven Enders, Citigroup Inc., Research Division).
  • Management tone was slightly positive and explanatory, emphasizing predictability/governance in AI adoption and reiterating second-half weighting; CFO Stillwell framed Q1 as expected seasonality: “the rhythm of our business was expected to return to a more typical seasonal pattern in the Q1 of 2026.”
  • Versus the prior quarter’s more celebratory tone around “an outstanding year” and guidance issuance, the current quarter carried more caveats on comparisons and macro frictions while continuing to project confidence in cloud mix and pipeline.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Q4 2025 centered on full-year performance, legal overhang removal, and issuing annual guidance; Q1 2026 emphasized seasonality, a tougher comparison to “the $60 million of net ACV add in the first quarter of 2025,” and conversion timing. (COO & CFO Kenneth Stillwell)
  • Q4 2025 highlighted annual targets such as “Total revenue of $2 billion” and “free cash flow of $575 million”; Q1 2026 reiterated the policy of not updating annual guidance and discussed a back-end loaded renewal portfolio and second-half term license weighting. (COO & CFO Stillwell)
  • Analyst focus shifted from confidence in the 15% ACV growth guide and macro deal environment in Q4 to Q1 questions around Q1 ACV level, maintenance decline visibility, government delays, and AI operating-cost scrutiny.

Risks and Concerns

  • “the federal government shutdown, 2 wars… clearly puts pressure on the entire environment.” (COO & CFO Kenneth Stillwell)
  • “there were a few deals and a renewal or 2 that actually did slip out of Q1” tied to government procurement changes; management mitigation centered on expectation that delays are timing-related and that “we have cloud choice… to work with different hyperscalers in regions.” (COO & CFO Stillwell)
  • “there's a potential for a derisking… because of the impact of higher oil prices, temporary inflation, goods flow being disrupted.” (CFO Stillwell)

Final Takeaway

Management characterized Q1 2026 as a seasonally normal quarter with a difficult prior-year comparison, while pointing investors to Pega Cloud’s growth, a rapidly rising cloud ACV mix, and strong Q1 free cash flow alongside substantial capital returns. The company repeatedly framed Blueprint as the driver of unusually high new-logo pipeline creation and expected second-half ACV conversion, while also flagging near-term revenue and ACV pressure in maintenance and term licenses as the cloud mix continues to rise toward the stated 75%+ long-term goal.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: U.S. video game spending jumped 12% YoY in March - Circana
data.description:
U.S. video game spending jumped 12% year-over-year in March to $5.3B, with new titles including Crimson Desert, MLB The Show 26, and Pokémon Pokopia fueling the growth, according to data from market research firm Circana.

Hardware spending in March grew by 69% when compared to a year ago, to $500M, the report said, adding that accessories spending grew by 5% when compared to a year ago, to $252M.

Nintendo Switch 2 was the best-selling hardware platform across units and dollars for both March and 2026 year-to-date, with PlayStation 5 ranking 2nd across both measures and time periods, Circana said.

MLB The Show 26 took the top spot in March for the best-selling title, followed by Resident Evil: Requie.

Take-Two Interactive’s (TTWO) WWE 2K26 and NBA 2K26, Microsoft’s (MSFT) Call of Duty: Black Ops 7, EA Sports FC 26 published by Electronic Arts (EA), Sony's (SONY) Marathon (2026), and Nintendo’s (NTDOF)(NTDOY) newly launched Pokémon Pokopia also made it to the top 10 list for the month.

In mobile content, Sensor Tower reported MONOPOLY GO!, Last War: Survival, Royal Match, Candy Crush Saga, and Pokémon GO were among the top games by U.S. consumer spending in March.

“While Pokémon GO maintained its momentum from the high performing Road to Kalos event in late February, this performance is expected to cool off in April,” said Bryan Isagholian of Sensor Tower.

data.title: Market breadth is driving the current rally in stocks – strategist
data.description:
Market breadth, not just technology stocks, is driving the current rally in equities, according to Brian Levitt, chief global market strategist at Invesco.

In an interview with CNBC, Levitt emphasized that the broadening of market (SP500), (COMP:IND), (DJI) gains across multiple sectors signals a healthier investment environment than one dominated solely by tech leadership.

The renewed attention on technology stocks (VGT), (XLK), (IYW) is part of what Levitt described as a “structural secular trend” in the market.

While semiconductors (SMH), (SOXX), (SOXL) were historically viewed as cyclical, earnings have continued to deliver, and forward valuations remain reasonable.

Given the concentration of tech giants in major indices, Levitt acknowledged that “the concentration in some of these indices will make it hard for the markets to go down without tech leadership.”

However, the strategist pointed to encouraging signs beyond Silicon Valley. Since March, emerging markets (IEMG), (VWO), (EEM), mid-caps (IJH), (VO), (IWR), (MDY), and cyclicals have all posted gains.

“It’s not just tech that’s leading this. It is a broadening of the market, which tends to be healthier,” Levitt said. This diversification aligns with expectations from the start of the year as major global economies began expanding.

Small-cap stocks (IJR), (IWM), (VB), (VBR), (VBK) are also breaking out amid renewed optimism about the global economic expansion and the prospect of lower interest rates from the Federal Reserve.

Levitt noted that despite shifting expectations around rate cuts following the war in Iran, credit markets remained stable—a testament to fundamental economic health. Earnings for small businesses, he added, “have been quite good.”

The market has demonstrated remarkable resilience in the face of geopolitical turmoil. Unlike the 2022 Russian invasion of Ukraine, which coincided with inflation already running at 7%, the current environment features contained inflation and solid fundamentals.

“It’s a market that has chosen to look past it quite quickly,” Levitt said regarding the conflict in Iran.

Key economic indicators support the bullish outlook: credit spreads have tightened, five-year inflation breakevens remain stable, and the dollar (DXY) is weakening.

Levitt observed that the U.S. is no longer as energy-intensive an economy as it once was, which has insulated markets from oil supply concerns. The data suggests conditions are significantly better than many anticipated just weeks ago.

data.title: Stock selection is the way to go as markets experience continued volatility - Raymond James’s Larry Adam
data.description:
Stock selection is now critical for investors navigating heightened market volatility driven by Federal Reserve leadership changes and the midterm election cycle, according to Larry Adam, chief investment officer at Raymond James.

In an interview with CNBC, Adam pointed to historical precedent, noting that Fed chairmanship transitions have consistently triggered market turbulence—from Black Monday occurring within two months of Alan Greenspan taking office to the “Vomageddon” volatility spike on Powell’s first day.

Combined with the typical underperformance seen during midterm election years, Adam urged investors to be highly selective in their positioning.

Technology (VGT), (XLK), (IYW) stands out as Adam’s favored sector amid the uncertain backdrop. He cited the sector’s impressive 40% earnings growth and valuations trading below their 10-year averages, describing it as offering “the lowest PEG ratio that we’ve seen.”

Adam expressed particular caution around energy (XLE), (AMLP), (VDE) and consumer staples (XLP), (VDC), (IYK).

He predicted energy prices could decline 30%-40% to around $60 per barrel by year-end, calling this “a big headwind in front of that sector.”

Consumer staples (XLP) present a similarly unfavorable risk-reward profile, with valuations comparable to technology but earnings growth of just 6%—roughly one-seventh of what tech delivers.

When it comes to market capitalization, Adam favors large-cap stocks over small caps, emphasizing that “you get a lot more earnings growth in that sector.”

Large caps also benefit from superior technology exposure at 32% compared to just 13% for small caps (IJR), (IWM), (VB), while small caps carry heavier exposure to the energy sector he views negatively.

The strategist maintains a clear preference for U.S. equities over developed international markets.

“The U.S. economy is showing that it’s a lot more resilient,” Adam observed, pointing to weakness in luxury retail sales in Europe and Japan as evidence of diverging economic fortunes.

Policy divergence further supports Adam’s bullish U.S. outlook. While he expects the Federal Reserve to cut rates just once this year, he anticipates the Bank of Japan and European Central Bank will raise rates.

This combination of stronger fundamentals, superior earnings growth, and more accommodative monetary policy leads Adam to conclude that U.S. markets will outperform their international counterparts in the year ahead.

U.S. markets tracking ETFs: (DIA), (DDM), (DOG), (DXD), (SDOW), (SPY), (VOO), (IVV), (RSP), (SSO), (UPRO), (SH), (SDS), (SPXU), (QQQ), (QQQM), (TQQQ), (QID), and (SQQQ).

data.title: Prudential outlines Japan sales suspension's financial impact
data.description:
Prudential Financial (PRU) outlined the expected financial impact from the extended suspension of new sales at its Prudential Life Insurance Company of Japan ((POJ)) unit to investors late on Tuesday.

The company earlier reported that it's extending by 180 days the initial 90-day suspension of new sales at POJ as it works to restructure the unit's governance, sales practices, and oversight. Prudential (PRU) stock fell 1.7% in late Wednesday trading.

The life insurance and retirement account company expects a $525M-$575M impact on pretax adjusted operating income in 2026 and a $400M-$450M impact on 2027 pretax adjusted operating income. Those amounts include the $300M-$350M impact, disclosed in February, expected from the initial 90-day suspension.

The dollar amounts primarily reflect the annualized impact of surrenders and the suspension of new sales activity for most of 2026, as well as the cost of restarting new sales in 2027, Chairman, CEO, and President Andrew Sullivan said on the investor call.

"During the extended suspension period, our focus will be on sustained, measurable actions designed to improve customer outcomes," Sullivan said. "This includes strengthening POJ's oversight and governance, improving the transparency of sales activities, and enhancing protections around customer information."

The estimated financial impact on Prudential's Japan earnings is ~18%, and on Prudential Financial's (PRU) is ~8%, the company said. More than 90% of POJ's pretax adjusted operating income is driven by its in-force business, it emphasized.

As part of the process to restore trust with its customers, Prudential (PRU) has formed an independent third-party customer reimbursement committee. The company has funded the committee with $70M.

"We believe the underlying fundamentals of the franchise, its customer base, its capabilities, and our long-term market presence and commitment remain intact," Sullivan said.

Prudential's (PRU) businesses in Japan consist of Prudential Life Insurance of Japan ((POJ)), Gibraltar Life Insurance Company, and Prudential Gibraltar Life Insurance ((PGFL)). The suspension of new sales only applies to POJ. However, the company is undertaking a proactive review of Gibraltar, which has 3M customers. That unit has received 70 inquiries about potential reimbursements. "To be clear, those are inbound inquiries," Sullivan said. "They're not confirmed cases of misconduct."
